div.h2: seq_file Mechanics: Why proc_read Breaks vs. Why seq_file Works

div.p: Key Rule: Call proc_create("mydevice", 0444, NULL, &mydevice_proc_ops) in mydevice_init. Call proc_remove(proc_entry) first in mydevice_exit — before kfree(kernel_buffer) — because a concurrent cat reader may still be inside mydevice_proc_show accessing buffer_data_len. proc_remove blocks until all readers finish. On kernel ≥ 5.6: use struct proc_ops. On kernel < 5.6: use struct file_operations for /proc entries.

strong.code: proc_read(buf, start, offset, count, eof, data)

ul.li: offset = byte position to resume from

div.p: ⚠ OUTPUT > 4096 BYTES? sprintf overwrites beyond buf end → buffer overflow in kernel memory → OR snprintf silently truncates at 4096 → userspace cat sees incomplete data → no error returned — silent failure
When userspace calls read(fd, buf, 512) multiple times: offset parameter must track position. Driver must manually slice output string. Historical bugs (thousands of drivers):

ul.li: cat hangs or produces garbled output

div.p: Fixed-size buffer + manual offset tracking is fundamentally incompatible with arbitrary-length kernel output delivered in arbitrary-sized userspace read() chunks. This API was deprecated. Do not use it.

single_open(filp, mydevice_proc_show, NULL) Allocates struct seq_file on the heap. Stores in filp->private_data. c struct seq_file { char buf; / dynamic page list / size_t size; / current buf size / size_t from; / offset within buf / size_t count; / bytes in buf / loff_t index; / iterator position */ void private; / = NULL (our data) */ struct seq_operations op; / start/next/stop/show */ };
Called by VFS on every read(fd, buf, N) userspace call. Internal logic:
Handles any N transparently. No driver code needed for fragmentation.
Never check seq_printf return value. Buffer overflow is handled internally by allocating additional pages.

ol.li: If remaining space < needed bytes: → seq_buf_alloc() doubles buffer size → copies existing content to new allocation → retries the printf
m->count += bytes_written Result: buffer grows to fit any output. No driver-visible size limit. No driver-visible truncation.

div.p: cat /proc/mydevice calls read() with 4096-byte buf: → seq_read calls show() once → ~80 bytes generated → copies 80 bytes → userspace → next read() returns 0 → cat exits ✓ dd if=/proc/mydevice bs=1 calls read() 1 byte at a time: → seq_read calls show() once → 80 bytes in internal buf → delivers 1 byte per call, tracking from offset → after 80 calls, returns 0 → dd exits ✓ No driver code changes needed between these cases.
Only proc_open and show() are yours. Everything else: kernel provides.
